content.full_text:
Re:
FnrB Polrrrcnl Pnecrrcss C oMMrssroN
:128 J Street r Suite 620 o Sacramento. CA 95814-2329
\916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
September 28,2010
Elaine W. Reagan
General Counsel, SDCERS
401 West A. Street, Suite 400
San Diego, CA 92101
Your Request for Advice
Our file No. A-10-143
Dear Ms. Reagan:
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Herb Morgan, Trustee of the
San Diego City Fmployees' Retirement System, regarding the provisions of the Political Reform
Act (the "Act").1 This letter should not be construed as assistance on any conduct that may have
already taken place. (See Regulation 18329(bX8XA).) In addition, this letter is based on the
facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a
finder of fact when it renders assistance. (ln re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Finally, please
note that we only offer advice under the Act and do not have the authority to offer advice on the
applicability, if any, of any local or federal rules that may apply to you or yollr question.
QUESTION
Is Herb Morgan, an appointed trustee on the Board of Administration for the San Diego
City Employees' Retirement System ("SDCERS") required to report as either a gift or income
payments that co-hosts of client events make to third party vendors?
CONCLUSION
No. The payments are not gifts or income to Mr. Morgan and are not reportable, nor are
they considered when determining whether the trustee has a conflict of interest in decision-
making. He might receive a reportable gift if he attends the event, however.
' The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
ref'erences are to the Covernment Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are sontained in Sections l8l l0 through I 8997 of Title 2 of the Califtrrnia Code of Regulations. All
regulatory ref'erences are to Title 2, Division 6 of the Calitbrnia Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.


File No. A-10-143
Page No. 2
FACTS
You represent Herb Morgan, a trustee appointed to serve on the Board of Administration
for the San Diego City Employees' Retirement System. Mr. Morgan is a public official and files
a Form 700, Statement of Economic [nterest. Mr. Morgan is also the CEO and owner of an
investment advisory firm, Efficient Market Advisors, LLC ("Efficient").
When Efficient hosts events for its clients, it incurs costs to rent facilities and food.
Other service providers (the "Providers") that have clients in common with Efficient participate
in and contribute to the costs of the events. The Providers contribute to the cost of the events by
tendering payment directly to the vendor providing event services. The Providers have staff at
the events. The Providers do not have business with SDCERS.
A1\ALYSIS
Income
The Act defines income at section 82030(a), as follows:
"(a) 'Income' means [. . .] a payment received, including but not limited to
any salary, wage, advance, dividend, interest, rent, proceeds from any sale, gift,
including any gift of food or beverage, loan, forgiveness or payment of
indebtedness received by the filer, reimbursement for expenses, per diem, or
contribution to an insurance or pension program paid by any person other than an
employer, and including any community property interest in the income of a
spouse."
Mr. Morgan is not receiving any payments or reimbursements. He is co-hosting an event
where he pays a portion of the cost and others pay aportion. These payments flow directly to
third party vendors and provide an equal benefit to Mr. Morgan and the Providers. Because there
is no payment that Mr. Morgan receives, there is no income.
Gift
The term "gift" is defined in Section 82028(a) as:
"Any payment that confers a personal benefit on the recipient, to the extent that
consideration of equal or greater value is not received and includes a rebate or
discount in the price of anything of value unless the rebate or discount is made in
the regular course of business to members of the public without regard to official
status."
The payments that the Providers will make to the third party vendors do not
confer a personal benefit on Mr. Morgan. The Providers are acting in their own interests
and paying for services received.


File No. A-10-143
Page No. 3
While these particular payments are not gifts, Mr. Morgan may receive a gift
should he personally attend an event. The gift, free admission to an event paid for by
another, would be the total amount of the event divided by the number of people
attending. This number gives an approximate "ticket amount" for the event. (See
Regulation 18946.2(c), copy enclosed.) To determine the amount of the gift of each
individual Provider, you would divide the cost of the "ticket" by the total number of
Providers, including yourself. The reportable gift from each Provider would be that
number. This is subject to reporting thresholds and gift limits.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (9L6) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
ut U![t^,," a
By: Heather M. Rowan
Counsel, Legal Division
HMRjgl
Enclosure

No. The payments are not gifts or income to Mr. Morgan and are not reportable, nor are
they considered when determining whether the trustee has a conflict of interest in decision-
making. He might receive a reportable gift if he attends the event, however.
'The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are sontained in Sections l8l l0 through I 8997 of Title 2 of the Califtrrnia Code of Regulations. All
regulatory ref'erences are to Title 2, Division 6 of the Calitbrnia Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

CONCLUSION: No. The payments are not gifts or income to Mr. Morgan and are not reportable, nor are
